title: Argument Labels 🏷️
description: Swift's secret to making code read like English.

slides.title: Poetry in Motion 📖
slides.content:
# Reading like a Sentence

Swift is famous for making its code read like plain English. However, sometimes parameters make code rigid.

Take this example:
`func displayMessage(message: String, name: String)`.
Calling it looks like:
`displayMessage(message: "Hi", name: "Tim")`.

That's a bit clunky. Swift lets us fix this using **Argument Labels**.

slides.title: Two Names for One Parameter 🎭

quizData.question: If you write `func greet(person name: String)`, what name do you use to call the function?
quizData.explanation: The FIRST name ('person') is the external label used when calling. The SECOND name ('name') is used inside the function.

slides.title: Omitting Labels Completely 🚫

quizData.question: Why do developers use an underscore `_` before a parameter?

options.text: To hide the parameter label when calling the function.

quizData.explanation: The underscore `_` acts as a blank external label, meaning the caller doesn't have to type out the parameter name.

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: Look at how cleanly this function reads when called, almost like reading a book!
